addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Let me read the paddock before the docket — the gazette has both.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣Bb
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene grown into the ACG audit program, in two strata.
6	First, targeted allocation sweeps feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP
7	feature finalization — magic-string -> constant sweeps and noun-cluster
8	curation, each with a blast radius that merges trivially.
9	Second, the audit program: per-pair spec/module audits under the ACG move
10	catalog, run while ACG is young so the guide and the documents harden together.
11	The first pilot grew the program a third stratum:
12	mechanism and guide work the audits themselves surfaced —
13	the cited-constraint anchor design,
14	the jailer dialect guide,
15	and the deferred collapse landings they gate.
16	A seed, not a tree: scope stays tight to the source/spec allocation family.
17	Resist becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.
18	
19	## Governing idea
20	
21	The authority is ACG (Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/ACG-AllocationCodingGuide.md) —
22	the spine, the three homes, the move discipline, and the numbered move catalog
23	all live there.
24	This paddock cites and never restates
25	(ACG's statements -> definition-sites clause applied to itself;
26	an earlier revision of this paddock paraphrased the guide and has been drained).
27	
28	## Cinched
29	
30	- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
31	  authoring/allocation; asciidoc is MCM's domain).
32	  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG.
33	  Not released at MVP.
34	- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practice; no mandated universals;
35	  no qualify-time enforcement.
36	  Universals are candidates confirmed pace-by-pace.
37	- Audit pace shape: one pace = one spec-subdocument/bash-module pair,
38	  source-side and spec-side moves applied in one read;
39	  the inherently cross-document moves (paraphrase collapse, tidemark drain)
40	  get single corpus-wide sweep paces instead.
41	- Pilot posture: ACG is untested — three pilots run before any roster of pair
42	  paces is slated.
43	  Every pilot's deliverable is twofold: the findings,
44	  and the ACG amendments where the guide proved vague, wrong, or silent.
45	  Roster vs further pilots is a post-pilot groom decision.
46	- Pilot selection (groom survey): the sentry pair tests the edit-time/Palisade
47	  comment boundary;
48	  the access-probe pair carries known-positive paraphrase/ornament specimens,
49	  so the audit proves itself against a known answer;
50	  the conjure pair is the richest surface and the scale test,
51	  gated with the rest of the ₣BH lane.
52	- Pilots execute at top model tier in the operator's chat;
53	  delegation to lesser tiers is for the post-pilot roster only,
54	  after ACG hardens.
55	- Per-pace model tier rides in each docket's Character line
56	  (judgment/curation work at opus, the mechanical sweeps at sonnet);
57	  the pilot top-tier gate stays a hard Gate, not a Character hint.
58	- Antipattern catalog: mcm_antipattern broadens to cover delivery defects as
59	  well as document defects;
60	  Stun, Molehill, and word cancer all home in MCM's Antipatterns section,
61	  with ACG and the JJK gradient-delivery guidance citing rather than defining.
62	- Heat tipped toward guidance over code repair (operator ruling at pilot 1):
63	  the pilot verdict was a mature spec side and a double-written source side,
64	  so guide and mechanism quality is the multiplier;
65	  collapse/relocation landings defer behind the cited-constraint anchor
66	  mechanism so each residue lands once, citing an anchor.
67	  Pilot 1's rulings live in ACG, not here:
68	  the citation-collapse case on ACGm_104,
69	  the Palisade fallback bound in the three-homes section,
70	  the cited-constraint anchor deferred record with its two binding rulings,
71	  and the one-file-one-guide rule in Related Guides.
72	- Remaining pilots apply the citation-collapse pattern where it fits rather
73	  than re-deriving it; their amendment hunting aims at ground pilot 1 did not
74	  cover.
75	- The jailer dialect (zero-dependency in-vessel POSIX sh) gets its own guide,
76	  promoted ahead of rule-of-3;
77	  rationale recorded in the pilot findings memo
78	  (Memos/memo-20260612-acg-pilot-sentry-findings.md).
79	- Motivating strategy (operator): regenerate-from-specs trials — cheaper
80	  models regenerating code from spec + guide, diffed against live code — as
81	  the future forcing function on guide/spec quality.
82	  Makes the dedup direction a prerequisite, not hygiene;
83	  shapes prioritization toward guides and mechanisms.
84	
85	## Sequencing & lanes
86	
87	Ungated front, in order: the access-probe pilot next,
88	then the cited-constraint anchor design,
89	then the jailer dialect guide,
90	then the sentry collapse landings the anchor mechanism gates.
91	The BUK test-vocab mint and the axvo symbol-lookahead amendment ride the back
92	of the ungated front:
93	the mint is self-contained in BUS0,
94	and the axvo pace is only partially gated —
95	its RBS0 migration share defers until ₣BH lands.
96	
97	The rest of the heat is gated on ₣BH:
98	
99	- Eviction (removing superseded terms) — ₣BH curates the KILL/KEEP list;
100	  the standing conformance engine's rows populate per-cluster behind ₣BH's
101	  cutovers.
102	  ACG names that engine the canonical home for evicted-term assertions.
103	- The conjure pilot — its surface is under active ₣BH rewrite;
104	  findings against it now would go stale.
105	- The rbrv.env single-home sweeps and the noun-cluster curation —
106	  their files share ₣BH's rewrite surface.
107	- Cluster repair (curating living vocabulary to a single home) is disjoint from
108	  eviction and never removes terms.
109	
110	Triage rider across all audit work:
111	Manor / Depot / account-role vocabulary is ₣BZ's lane and
112	reliquary / Lode / Hallmark vocabulary is ₣BH's — report, never touch.
113	Verb clusters stay unslated until their owning heats land;
114	slating against terms two hot heats are still moving would re-create the drift
115	this heat exists to kill.
116	
117	## Done looks like
118	
119	ACG hardened through three audit pilots, amendments landed;
120	the antipattern catalog homed in MCM with citers repointed;
121	the cited-constraint anchor mechanism specified at its MCM/AXLA home;
122	the jailer dialect guide authored and registered;
123	the sentry collapses landed through the anchor mechanism and the pilot
124	findings memo retired;
125	the rbrv.env literal single-homed in bash and rust as the first worked
126	ACGm_101 application;
127	the noun clusters curated to single homes;
128	the post-pilot groom has decided roster vs further pilots.
129	
130	# jjezs_pace ₢BbAAO
131	
132	## Character
133	Design conversation requiring judgment — a new MCM/AXLA-level mechanism, worked at top tier.
134	
135	## Goal
136	Design the cited-constraint anchor:
137	a third census mechanism beside quoin and sprue —
138	a normative proposition (invariant, deliberate deviation, foreign signature)
139	that the spec defines once under a minted ID
140	and that code comments and tests cite,
141	so one grep returns definition, defending sites, and attacking tests.
142	Settle the MCM mechanics (new category, not a quoin variant),
143	the ID shape, the allocation path, the category taxonomy,
144	and the validator rules this feeds.
145	ACG's "The cited-constraint anchor (deferred)" section is the entry point and
146	gets repointed from deferred to live when this lands.
147	
148	## Cinched
149	- Direction is cinched; the design leanings are not.
150	  Leanings recorded in Memos/memo-20260612-acg-pilot-sentry-findings.md
151	  (opaque dense suffix for the closed-spec/open-code boundary,
152	  category as definition-site field not ID letter,
153	  allocator and naming open) —
154	  confirm or overturn each at mount.
155	- Test names never appear in source comments (ACG ruling);
156	  this mechanism is the named replacement for that linkage.
157	
158	## Done when
159	The category is specified at its MCM/AXLA home;
160	ID shape and allocation path decided;
161	ACG's deferred section repointed;
162	the first anchors are mintable by the relocation work this pace gates.

# jjezs_paddock ₣Bb

## Shape

Pre-MVP source/spec hygiene grown into the ACG audit program, in two strata.
First, targeted allocation sweeps feathered in pace-at-a-time alongside MVP
feature finalization — magic-string -> constant sweeps and noun-cluster
curation, each with a blast radius that merges trivially.
Second, the audit program: per-pair spec/module audits under the ACG move
catalog, run while ACG is young so the guide and the documents harden together.
The first pilot grew the program a third stratum:
mechanism and guide work the audits themselves surfaced —
the cited-constraint anchor design,
the jailer dialect guide,
and the deferred collapse landings they gate.
A seed, not a tree: scope stays tight to the source/spec allocation family.
Resist becoming the junk drawer for unrelated cleanups.

## Governing idea

The authority is ACG (Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/ACG-AllocationCodingGuide.md) —
the spine, the three homes, the move discipline, and the numbered move catalog
all live there.
This paddock cites and never restates
(ACG's statements -> definition-sites clause applied to itself;
an earlier revision of this paddock paraphrased the guide and has been drained).

## Cinched

- ACG = "Allocation Coding Guide" (not "Asciidoc" — the guide governs source
  authoring/allocation; asciidoc is MCM's domain).
  Veiled/proprietary, guide-family sibling to BCG/RCG/WSG/CBG.
  Not released at MVP.
- v1 posture: bless and name emergent practice; no mandated universals;
  no qualify-time enforcement.
  Universals are candidates confirmed pace-by-pace.
- Audit pace shape: one pace = one spec-subdocument/bash-module pair,
  source-side and spec-side moves applied in one read;
  the inherently cross-document moves (paraphrase collapse, tidemark drain)
  get single corpus-wide sweep paces instead.
- Pilot posture: ACG is untested — three pilots run before any roster of pair
  paces is slated.
  Every pilot's deliverable is twofold: the findings,
  and the ACG amendments where the guide proved vague, wrong, or silent.
  Roster vs further pilots is a post-pilot groom decision.
- Pilot selection (groom survey): the sentry pair tests the edit-time/Palisade
  comment boundary;
  the access-probe pair carries known-positive paraphrase/ornament specimens,
  so the audit proves itself against a known answer;
  the conjure pair is the richest surface and the scale test,
  gated with the rest of the ₣BH lane.
- Pilots execute at top model tier in the operator's chat;
  delegation to lesser tiers is for the post-pilot roster only,
  after ACG hardens.
- Per-pace model tier rides in each docket's Character line
  (judgment/curation work at opus, the mechanical sweeps at sonnet);
  the pilot top-tier gate stays a hard Gate, not a Character hint.
- Antipattern catalog: mcm_antipattern broadens to cover delivery defects as
  well as document defects;
  Stun, Molehill, and word cancer all home in MCM's Antipatterns section,
  with ACG and the JJK gradient-delivery guidance citing rather than defining.
- Heat tipped toward guidance over code repair (operator ruling at pilot 1):
  the pilot verdict was a mature spec side and a double-written source side,
  so guide and mechanism quality is the multiplier;
  collapse/relocation landings defer behind the cited-constraint anchor
  mechanism so each residue lands once, citing an anchor.
  Pilot 1's rulings live in ACG, not here:
  the citation-collapse case on ACGm_104,
  the Palisade fallback bound in the three-homes section,
  the cited-constraint anchor deferred record with its two binding rulings,
  and the one-file-one-guide rule in Related Guides.
- Remaining pilots apply the citation-collapse pattern where it fits rather
  than re-deriving it; their amendment hunting aims at ground pilot 1 did not
  cover.
- The jailer dialect (zero-dependency in-vessel POSIX sh) gets its own guide,
  promoted ahead of rule-of-3;
  rationale recorded in the pilot findings memo
  (Memos/memo-20260612-acg-pilot-sentry-findings.md).
- Motivating strategy (operator): regenerate-from-specs trials — cheaper
  models regenerating code from spec + guide, diffed against live code — as
  the future forcing function on guide/spec quality.
  Makes the dedup direction a prerequisite, not hygiene;
  shapes prioritization toward guides and mechanisms.

## Sequencing & lanes

Ungated front, in order: the access-probe pilot next,
then the cited-constraint anchor design,
then the jailer dialect guide,
then the sentry collapse landings the anchor mechanism gates.
The BUK test-vocab mint and the axvo symbol-lookahead amendment ride the back
of the ungated front:
the mint is self-contained in BUS0,
and the axvo pace is only partially gated —
its RBS0 migration share defers until ₣BH lands.

The rest of the heat is gated on ₣BH:

- Eviction (removing superseded terms) — ₣BH curates the KILL/KEEP list;
  the standing conformance engine's rows populate per-cluster behind ₣BH's
  cutovers.
  ACG names that engine the canonical home for evicted-term assertions.
- The conjure pilot — its surface is under active ₣BH rewrite;
  findings against it now would go stale.
- The rbrv.env single-home sweeps and the noun-cluster curation —
  their files share ₣BH's rewrite surface.
- Cluster repair (curating living vocabulary to a single home) is disjoint from
  eviction and never removes terms.

Triage rider across all audit work:
Manor / Depot / account-role vocabulary is ₣BZ's lane and
reliquary / Lode / Hallmark vocabulary is ₣BH's — report, never touch.
Verb clusters stay unslated until their owning heats land;
slating against terms two hot heats are still moving would re-create the drift
this heat exists to kill.

## Done looks like

ACG hardened through three audit pilots, amendments landed;
the antipattern catalog homed in MCM with citers repointed;
the cited-constraint anchor mechanism specified at its MCM/AXLA home;
the jailer dialect guide authored and registered;
the sentry collapses landed through the anchor mechanism and the pilot
findings memo retired;
the rbrv.env literal single-homed in bash and rust as the first worked
ACGm_101 application;
the noun clusters curated to single homes;
the post-pilot groom has decided roster vs further pilots.

# jjezs_pace ₢BbAAO

## Character
Design conversation requiring judgment — a new MCM/AXLA-level mechanism, worked at top tier.

## Goal
Design the cited-constraint anchor:
a third census mechanism beside quoin and sprue —
a normative proposition (invariant, deliberate deviation, foreign signature)
that the spec defines once under a minted ID
and that code comments and tests cite,
so one grep returns definition, defending sites, and attacking tests.
Settle the MCM mechanics (new category, not a quoin variant),
the ID shape, the allocation path, the category taxonomy,
and the validator rules this feeds.
ACG's "The cited-constraint anchor (deferred)" section is the entry point and
gets repointed from deferred to live when this lands.

## Cinched
- Direction is cinched; the design leanings are not.
  Leanings recorded in Memos/memo-20260612-acg-pilot-sentry-findings.md
  (opaque dense suffix for the closed-spec/open-code boundary,
  category as definition-site field not ID letter,
  allocator and naming open) —
  confirm or overturn each at mount.
- Test names never appear in source comments (ACG ruling);
  this mechanism is the named replacement for that linkage.

## Done when
The category is specified at its MCM/AXLA home;
ID shape and allocation path decided;
ACG's deferred section repointed;
the first anchors are mintable by the relocation work this pace gates.

content.text: This pace is a **design conversation**, marked `[rough]`, at top tier. Before I shape the mount, let me ground myself in the two things the docket points at — the recorded leanings and ACG's deferred entry point — so my proposed approach isn't hollow.

content.content:
1	# ACG Pilot 1 — Sentry Pair Audit Findings
2	
3	## Origin
4	
5	First ACG audit pilot (₣Bb rbk-10-mvp-acg-scrub, pace ₢BbAAI), run 2026-06-12
6	at top tier in the operator's chat per the pace gate. Pair audited:
7	`rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh` (RBJS)
8	against RBSSR + RBSSS, with RBSPT / RBSII / RBSDS read as the receiving-home
9	surface (RBSSS cites `{scr_port_setup}` for the entry-port rules, so the
10	sibling specs are where relocations actually land).
11	
12	Line numbers below are pinned to commit `1a99c1d02f4`. This memo retires when
13	the relocation pace dispositions the findings.
14	
15	## Headline
16	
17	The sentry pair is **well-specified but double-written**. Every dense comment
18	in RBJS already has a spec home carrying nearly the same wording — six
19	cross-medium paraphrase forks, not six unhomed comments. The spec copies are
20	consistently equal or better (more general, version-scoped). The move needed
21	everywhere is citation-collapse, not relocation; ACGm_104 was amended
22	accordingly (see Rulings).
23	
24	## Findings
25	
26	Each finding: site, content, move-type, receiving home, and what the residue
27	keeps. All detect-only; nothing was touched in this pace.
28	
29	### F1 — Docker default-route ownership (rbjs_sentry.sh:53-57)
30	
31	Five-line comment: Docker assigns the default route to the alphabetically-first
32	attached network's gateway, compose `priority` is not honored, so sentry derives
33	the uplink gateway itself and installs its own route.
34	
35	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
36	- **Home:** RBSSS step 2 NOTEs — already carries the content, *more* precisely
37	  (engine 28.x / Desktop 28.x version scoping the comment lacks).
38	- **Residue:** Docker's route choice is not trusted — deliberate override; cite.
39	- **Also:** the phrase "don't-trust-Docker discipline" appears in both comment
40	  and RBSSS:25 — ACGm_105 corpus seed.
41	
42	### F2 — rp_filter loose mode (rbjs_sentry.sh:122-130)
43	
44	Nine-line comment: strict reverse-path filtering drops published-port ingress
45	at routing time (before the FORWARD chain sees it) because delivery interface
46	and reverse route mismatch; loose mode is the actual invariant; rp_filter is
47	not the load-bearing security control.
48	
49	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
50	- **Home:** RBSPT step 3 — near-verbatim, and generalized ("the framework's
51	  gateway" where the comment hardcodes Docker Desktop's 192.168.65.1).
52	- **Residue:** loose is deliberate; strict silently kills entry-port traffic
53	  before the firewall sees it — the trap is a security-minded editor
54	  "hardening" it back to 1. Highest-value tripwire of the six.
55	
56	### F3 — DNAT per-IP classification (rbjs_sentry.sh:133-147)
57	
58	Fifteen-line comment: entry-port DNAT excludes exactly the two enclave
59	container IPs via RETURN short-circuit, deliberately NOT whole-CIDR exclusion —
60	on linux Docker Engine the host attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with
61	an in-CIDR address, so whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host SYN.
62	States the invariant: enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port
63	via DNAT.
64	
65	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
66	- **Home:** RBSPT step 4 — carries the rationale and the invariant sentence,
67	  generalized.
68	- **Residue:** per-IP RETURN is deliberately not whole-CIDR (the
69	  empirically-broken "simplification"); cite. The per-IP rules are the site an
70	  agent is most likely to "clean up" wrongly.
71	- **Comment-only extras, each with its own disposition:**
72	  - "Empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times" —
73	    history-time provenance; home is the commit; drop (ACGm_107-adjacent).
74	  - Concrete IPs (192.168.65.1, 10.0.2.100) — spec's general wording is the
75	    durable form; drop.
76	  - Ifrit sortie names (`direct_sentry_probe`, `net_dnat_entry_reflection`) —
77	    test names never in comments (ruling landed in ACG); linkage rides the
78	    future cited-constraint anchor: spec defines the invariant, comment and
79	    sorties cite it.
80	- **Also:** the invariant sentence is worded in both RBJS:146 and RBSPT:35 —
81	  ACGm_105 corpus seed.
82	
83	### F4 — MASQUERADE return-path symmetry (rbjs_sentry.sh:157-160)
84	
85	Four-line comment: MASQUERADE rewrites the post-DNAT source to sentry's enclave
86	IP so the bottle's reply routes back through sentry and conntrack reverses the
87	NAT; without it replies traverse default-via-sentry into transit — empirically
88	broken on Docker Desktop.
89	
90	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
91	- **Home:** RBSPT step 6 — near-verbatim.
92	- **Residue:** the MASQUERADE is load-bearing for the reply path; cite.
93	- **Extra:** "per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic" — history-time
94	  provenance pointer; home is the commit/memo; drop (ACGm_107-adjacent).
95	
96	### F5 — conntrack-DNAT authorization (rbjs_sentry.sh:164-167)
97	
98	Four-line comment: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created
99	conntrack state for may forward; DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge;
100	return path handled by the parent FORWARD chain's RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT.
101	
102	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
103	- **Home:** RBSPT step 5 — near-verbatim including "unforgeable from either
104	  bridge." Cleanest pure duplicate of the six; no extras to salvage.
105	- **Residue:** the conntrack match IS the authorization; cite.
106	
107	### F6 — no-forwarding-path DNS comment (rbjs_sentry.sh:265-266)
108	
109	Two-line comment: no `server=` lines means no forwarding path exists; subdomain
110	queries return frozen IPs; all non-allowed domains get NXDOMAIN.
111	
112	- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
113	- **Home:** RBSDS resolve-then-freeze bullet — fuller than the comment (names
114	  the DNS subdomain exfiltration channel the design closes).
115	- **Residue:** the *absence* of `server=` lines is deliberate; cite. Purest
116	  case for an anchor citation — the trap is an absence, so no weird-looking
117	  line exists to hang suspicion on; an editor "fixing" missing forwarding
118	  re-opens the exfil channel.
119	
120	## Leftovers
121	
122	- **Spec side is clean (ACGm_106):** RBSPT / RBSII / RBSDS / RBSSR / RBSSS read
123	  as constraint throughout; no unmarked ornament worth draining. One
124	  clause-level nit: RBSSS states the sentry-takes-ownership doctrine twice in
125	  adjacent NOTEs (line 25 tail vs lines 26-28); one-line internal collapse,
126	  someday.
127	- **ACGm_107:** nothing in the specs; the two provenance pointers in RBJS are
128	  catalogued under F3/F4.
129	- **Non-ACG observation:** rbjs_sentry.sh lines 19-31 echo `RBJp0:` under the
130	  `RBJp1: Validate parameters` header — phase-label inconsistency in the
131	  startup announcements. Rides along with the relocation pace. Also the first
132	  catalogued violation of the unwritten jailer dialect rules — evidence for the
133	  jailer guide pace.
134	
135	## Corpus-sweep seeds (ACGm_105, for the later sweep paces)
136	
137	- Invariant sentence "enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port
138	  via DNAT": RBJS:146 and RBSPT:35.
139	- "don't-trust-Docker discipline": RBJS:55 and RBSSS:25.
140	- RBSSS-internal: ownership doctrine stated at line 25 tail and lines 26-28.
141	
142	## Rulings landed in ACG (this pace)
143	
144	1. **Citation-collapse** added to ACGm_104 as the named degenerate case —
145	   the pilot showed it is the common case where the spec side is mature.
146	2. **Palisade fallback bound** added to the three-homes section: spec is
147	   authoritative for Palisade characterization when a home exists; the comment
148	   keeps residue only (signature + tripwire + citation). Includes the context-
149	   economy division rule: code carries what an editor needs *before* deciding
150	   to load the spec; the spec carries everything after.
151	3. **Test names never in source comments** + the **cited-constraint anchor**
152	   recorded as a deferred MCM/AXLA-level mechanism, with the
153	   wait-don't-cite-twice sequencing rule for relocation sweeps.
154	4. **One file, one guide** added to Related Guides.
155	
156	## Cited-constraint anchor — design leanings (for the design pace)
157	
158	Direction agreed in the pilot conversation; mechanism deferred. Leanings to
159	confirm or overturn at mount — only the direction is cinched:
160	
161	- **Category taxonomy sketch** (from this pilot's specimens): *invariant*
162	  (must-hold property — F3/F5), *foreign-signature* (Palisade characterization
163	  — F1/F2's Docker behaviors), *deliberate-deviation* (don't-simplify tripwire
164	  — F2/F3/F6), *membrane* (Palisade workaround carrying a demolition date, per
165	  ROE conduct rule 5 — none in this pair, predicted category).
166	- **Opaque dense ID suffix** (firemark-flavored), not readable words: specs
167	  stay closed-source while code is released, so a readable anchor name leaks
168	  the security reasoning into open identifiers; an opaque token is inert. The
169	  tripwire *sentence* stays human-readable; only the anchor is opaque. Opaque
170	  IDs also carry near-zero rename pressure — no semantics to go stale — which
171	  keeps them out of cross-universe recension scope (diptych memo boundary).
172	- **Category as a definition-site field, not an ID letter:** firemark precedent
173	  (identity carries no semantics); recategorization must not remint released
174	  IDs; the validator answers grep-by-category by joining citations to
175	  definitions.
176	- **Allocator:** opaque IDs need collision-free minting; vvx is the precedent
177	  (jjx mints coronets). Open question for the design pace.
178	- **Validator linkage:** anchor rules join the diptych Phase-4 validator
179	  feedstock (memo-20260209-diptych-vision.md) — every code citation resolves to
180	  a spec definition; categories can carry per-category obligations (a membrane
181	  must name its demolition condition; an invariant must name a defender).
182	- **Naming:** the `RBs_`-style prefix and the category's minted word are
183	  design-pace work under full mint discipline (grep gate noted: lowercase
184	  `rbs*` is partially occupied; mixed-case anchors break the snake_case anchor
185	  convention — a deliberate break to record if taken).
186	
187	## Decisions of record
188	
189	- **Heat tipped toward guidance over code repair.** Pilot verdict: the guide
190	  needed amendments more than the script needs edits. Mechanical clincher: the
191	  six residues should cite anchors, so collapsing now with prose pointers means
192	  touching every site twice. Relocations deferred to a pace gated on the anchor
193	  mechanism.
194	- **Regenerate-from-specs strategy** (operator, this conversation): trials
195	  regenerating code from spec + guide with cheaper models, diffed against the
196	  live code, as the forcing function that measures guide/spec quality. Makes
197	  guides load-bearing infrastructure and the dedup direction a prerequisite,
198	  not hygiene. Anchor IDs localize regeneration failures.
199	- **Jailer dialect promoted to its own guide** despite rule-of-3: a guide is
200	  codification, not abstraction (low regret), and the regenerate strategy makes
201	  the dialect guide the generator's input for the security envelope — the code
202	  where an ungoverned agent "improvement" toward BCG idiom is most dangerous.
203	
204	## Disposition
205	
206	- ACG amendments: landed (this pace).
207	- CLAUDE.md memos-are-provenance test: landed (this pace).
208	- Cited-constraint anchor design: slated pace (₣Bb).
209	- Jailer dialect guide authoring: slated pace (₣Bb).
210	- Six citation-collapses + provenance drops + RBJp0/RBJp1 fix: slated pace
211	  (₣Bb), gated on the anchor mechanism; that pace retires this memo.
212	- ACGm_105 corpus seeds: held here for the corpus sweep paces.
213	- RBSSS internal nit: held here; rides any future RBSSS-touching pace.
214

### F2 — rp_filter loose mode (rbjs_sentry.sh:122-130)

not the load-bearing security control.

- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
- **Home:** RBSPT step 3 — near-verbatim, and generalized ("the framework's
  gateway" where the comment hardcodes Docker Desktop's 192.168.65.1).
- **Residue:** loose is deliberate; strict silently kills entry-port traffic
  before the firewall sees it — the trap is a security-minded editor
  "hardening" it back to 1. Highest-value tripwire of the six.

### F3 — DNAT per-IP classification (rbjs_sentry.sh:133-147)

Fifteen-line comment: entry-port DNAT excludes exactly the two enclave
container IPs via RETURN short-circuit, deliberately NOT whole-CIDR exclusion —
on linux Docker Engine the host attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with
an in-CIDR address, so whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host SYN.
States the invariant: enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port
via DNAT.

- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
- **Home:** RBSPT step 4 — carries the rationale and the invariant sentence,
  generalized.
- **Residue:** per-IP RETURN is deliberately not whole-CIDR (the
  empirically-broken "simplification"); cite. The per-IP rules are the site an
  agent is most likely to "clean up" wrongly.
- **Comment-only extras, each with its own disposition:**
  - "Empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times" —
    history-time provenance; home is the commit; drop (ACGm_107-adjacent).
  - Concrete IPs (192.168.65.1, 10.0.2.100) — spec's general wording is the
    durable form; drop.
  - Ifrit sortie names (`direct_sentry_probe`, `net_dnat_entry_reflection`) —
    test names never in comments (ruling landed in ACG); linkage rides the
    future cited-constraint anchor: spec defines the invariant, comment and
    sorties cite it.
- **Also:** the invariant sentence is worded in both RBJS:146 and RBSPT:35 —
  ACGm_105 corpus seed.

### F4 — MASQUERADE return-path symmetry (rbjs_sentry.sh:157-160)

Four-line comment: MASQUERADE rewrites the post-DNAT source to sentry's enclave
IP so the bottle's reply routes back through sentry and conntrack reverses the
NAT; without it replies traverse default-via-sentry into transit — empirically
broken on Docker Desktop.

- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
- **Home:** RBSPT step 6 — near-verbatim.
- **Residue:** the MASQUERADE is load-bearing for the reply path; cite.
- **Extra:** "per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic" — history-time
  provenance pointer; home is the commit/memo; drop (ACGm_107-adjacent).

### F5 — conntrack-DNAT authorization (rbjs_sentry.sh:164-167)

Four-line comment: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created
conntrack state for may forward; DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge;
return path handled by the parent FORWARD chain's RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT.

- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
- **Home:** RBSPT step 5 — near-verbatim including "unforgeable from either
  bridge." Cleanest pure duplicate of the six; no extras to salvage.
- **Residue:** the conntrack match IS the authorization; cite.

### F6 — no-forwarding-path DNS comment (rbjs_sentry.sh:265-266)

Two-line comment: no `server=` lines means no forwarding path exists; subdomain
queries return frozen IPs; all non-allowed domains get NXDOMAIN.

- **Move:** ACGm_104 citation-collapse.
- **Home:** RBSDS resolve-then-freeze bullet — fuller than the comment (names
  the DNS subdomain exfiltration channel the design closes).
- **Residue:** the *absence* of `server=` lines is deliberate; cite. Purest
  case for an anchor citation — the trap is an absence, so no weird-looking
  line exists to hang suspicion on; an editor "fixing" missing forwarding
  re-opens the exfil channel.

## Leftovers

- **Spec side is clean (ACGm_106):** RBSPT / RBSII / RBSDS / RBSSR / RBSSS read
  as constraint throughout; no unmarked ornament worth draining. One
  clause-level nit: RBSSS states the sentry-takes-ownership doctrine twice in
  adjacent NOTEs (line 25 tail vs lines 26-28); one-line internal collapse,
  someday.
- **ACGm_107:** nothing in the specs; the two provenance pointers in RBJS are
  catalogued under F3/F4.
- **Non-ACG observation:** rbjs_sentry.sh lines 19-31 echo `RBJp0:` under the
  `RBJp1: Validate parameters` header — phase-label inconsistency in the
  startup announcements. Rides along with the relocation pace. Also the first
  catalogued violation of the unwritten jailer dialect rules — evidence for the
  jailer guide pace.

## Corpus-sweep seeds (ACGm_105, for the later sweep paces)

- Invariant sentence "enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port
  via DNAT": RBJS:146 and RBSPT:35.
- "don't-trust-Docker discipline": RBJS:55 and RBSSS:25.
- RBSSS-internal: ownership doctrine stated at line 25 tail and lines 26-28.

## Rulings landed in ACG (this pace)

1. **Citation-collapse** added to ACGm_104 as the named degenerate case —
   the pilot showed it is the common case where the spec side is mature.
2. **Palisade fallback bound** added to the three-homes section: spec is
   authoritative for Palisade characterization when a home exists; the comment
   keeps residue only (signature + tripwire + citation). Includes the context-
   economy division rule: code carries what an editor needs *before* deciding
   to load the spec; the spec carries everything after.
3. **Test names never in source comments** + the **cited-constraint anchor**
   recorded as a deferred MCM/AXLA-level mechanism, with the
   wait-don't-cite-twice sequencing rule for relocation sweeps.
4. **One file, one guide** added to Related Guides.

## Cited-constraint anchor — design leanings (for the design pace)

Direction agreed in the pilot conversation; mechanism deferred. Leanings to
confirm or overturn at mount — only the direction is cinched:

- **Category taxonomy sketch** (from this pilot's specimens): *invariant*
  (must-hold property — F3/F5), *foreign-signature* (Palisade characterization
  — F1/F2's Docker behaviors), *deliberate-deviation* (don't-simplify tripwire
  — F2/F3/F6), *membrane* (Palisade workaround carrying a demolition date, per
  ROE conduct rule 5 — none in this pair, predicted category).
- **Opaque dense ID suffix** (firemark-flavored), not readable words: specs
  stay closed-source while code is released, so a readable anchor name leaks
  the security reasoning into open identifiers; an opaque token is inert. The
  tripwire *sentence* stays human-readable; only the anchor is opaque. Opaque
  IDs also carry near-zero rename pressure — no semantics to go stale — which
  keeps them out of cross-universe recension scope (diptych memo boundary).
- **Category as a definition-site field, not an ID letter:** firemark precedent
  (identity carries no semantics); recategorization must not remint released
  IDs; the validator answers grep-by-category by joining citations to
  definitions.
- **Allocator:** opaque IDs need collision-free minting; vvx is the precedent
  (jjx mints coronets). Open question for the design pace.
- **Validator linkage:** anchor rules join the diptych Phase-4 validator
  feedstock (memo-20260209-diptych-vision.md) — every code citation resolves to
  a spec definition; categories can carry per-category obligations (a membrane
  must name its demolition condition; an invariant must name a defender).
- **Naming:** the `RBs_`-style prefix and the category's minted word are
  design-pace work under full mint discipline (grep gate noted: lowercase
  `rbs*` is partially occupied; mixed-case anchors break the snake_case anchor
  convention — a deliberate break to record if taken).

## Decisions of record

- **Heat tipped toward guidance over code repair.** Pilot verdict: the guide
  needed amendments more than the script needs edits. Mechanical clincher: the
  six residues should cite anchors, so collapsing now with prose pointers means
  touching every site twice. Relocations deferred to a pace gated on the anchor
  mechanism.
- **Regenerate-from-specs strategy** (operator, this conversation): trials
  regenerating code from spec + guide with cheaper models, diffed against the
  live code, as the forcing function that measures guide/spec quality. Makes
  guides load-bearing infrastructure and the dedup direction a prerequisite,
  not hygiene. Anchor IDs localize regeneration failures.
- **Jailer dialect promoted to its own guide** despite rule-of-3: a guide is
  codification, not abstraction (low regret), and the regenerate strategy makes
  the dialect guide the generator's input for the security envelope — the code
  where an ungoverned agent "improvement" toward BCG idiom is most dangerous.

## Disposition

- ACG amendments: landed (this pace).
- CLAUDE.md memos-are-provenance test: landed (this pace).
- Cited-constraint anchor design: slated pace (₣Bb).
- Jailer dialect guide authoring: slated pace (₣Bb).
- Six citation-collapses + provenance drops + RBJp0/RBJp1 fix: slated pace
  (₣Bb), gated on the anchor mechanism; that pace retires this memo.
- ACGm_105 corpus seeds: held here for the corpus sweep paces.
- RBSSS internal nit: held here; rides any future RBSSS-touching pace.

### Name-identity's symbol-link dependency (deferred)

ACGm_102 becomes mechanically decidable only when an operation voicing carries its
implementing symbol explicitly. The surveyed shape: add a one-`mcm_inlay`
lookahead to `axvo_procedure` / `axvo_method` reading the literal source symbol
(the MCM-correct token for a prefix-disciplined name, matching how
`axhems_scoped_method` already reads an implementation method name). It is an
*amendment* to two established voicings with many live consumers, not a fresh
mint, so it carries a real migration and is held for its own deliberate pace
rather than folded into guide authoring. Recorded here as ACGm_102's named
dependency; raised and deferred, not dropped.

### The cited-constraint anchor (deferred)

The sentry-pair pilot surfaced a third census mechanism beside the quoin and the
sprue: a **cited constraint** — a normative proposition (an invariant, a
deliberate deviation, a foreign-behavior signature) that the spec defines once
under a minted ID and that code comments and tests cite. One grep then returns
the definition, every defending code site, and every attacking test — with no
registry to drift. The mechanism is MCM/AXLA-level design (a new category, not a
quoin variant) and is deferred to its own pace; two rulings bind now:

- **Test names never appear in source comments.** A comment naming its defending
  tests goes stale on every test rename with no forcing function. The linkage
  inverts: the test cites the constraint it defends.
- **Tripwire residues cite the constraint anchor once it exists.** Until then a
  residue names the spec section in prose — and relocation sweeps that would
  write prose pointers at scale wait for the mechanism rather than landing every
  citation twice.

## Related Guides

Which guide governs a file is itself an allocation question with a single
answer: **one file answers to exactly one guide, chosen by its environment.**
Guides may overlap in content; a file is never subject to two.

- **BCG** — host bash. Constant Discipline and Interface Contamination are the
  bash instances of the spine.
- **RCG** — host Rust. Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings), String Boundary
  Discipline, and Interface Contamination are the Rust instances.
- **WSG**, **CBG** — foreign-environment siblings (Windows transport, cloud step).
- **MCM** — builds the named homes ACG references; the design-time home.
- **AXLA** — the motif vocabulary; the lookahead scanning rule and the
  name-identity symbol-link dependency live against it.
- **CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" (mint)** — governs the naming homes
  themselves.

## Acronym Registry

| Term | Expansion |
|------|-----------|
| ACG | Allocation Coding Guide (this document) |
| The spine | "Reference the home" — values → constants, concepts → quoin-refs, statements → definition sites |
| Wire format | An RB-authored JSON document with senders and receivers; a named home whose keys are minted (sprued) names — the fourth clause |
| Word cancer | Variations of one normative idea scattered as paraphrases, each drifting independently; the metastatic violation of statements → definition sites. Quoin home: MCM `mcm_word_cancer` |
| Skidmark | Version commentary stranded in a durable document; history-time knowledge whose home is the commit. Quoin home: MCM `mcm_skidmark` |
| Home | The one right location for a fact, fixed by when it is read (design / edit / execution time) |
| Move-type | A catalogued `ACGm_` cleanup move: detect-rule, authority, licensing, verifier, countable Done |
| mutate-now | Licensing: a wrong move is cheaply caught, so the verb may repair in place |
| detect-only | Licensing: the verb reads and reports; no repair until a verifier exists |
| Name-identity | Concept linkage where the implementing symbol *is* the link to its quoin |
| Citation-collapse | ACGm_104's degenerate case: the receiving home already carries the comment's content, so the comment shrinks to residue and nothing relocates |
| Residue | What survives at the code after a collapse: the one-line signature, the do-not-"fix" tripwire, and the citation |
| Cited constraint | Deferred third census mechanism beside quoin and sprue: a normative proposition the spec defines under a minted ID and that code and tests cite (see The AXLA/MCM interface) |

content.text:
I'm oriented. Here's the mount.

---

**₢BbAAO — cited-constraint-anchor-design**

**The goal in one sentence:** design a *third census mechanism* beside the quoin and the sprue — a **cited constraint**: a normative proposition (an invariant, a deliberate deviation, a foreign-behavior signature) that a spec defines once under a minted ID, and that code comments and tests *cite* — so one grep returns the definition, every defending code site, and every attacking test, with no registry to drift.

**Where it sits in ₣Bb:** this is the keystone of the heat's ungated front. The sentry pilot found six residues that all want to cite an anchor; this pace is what lets them land *once* instead of being touched twice. The jailer guide and the sentry collapse landings sequence behind it.

**Silks:** `cited-constraint-anchor-design` fits the docket cleanly — no rename.

**Character:** design conversation, top tier (we're on opus — single-threaded, no delegation; the paddock gates pilots to your chat anyway).

The docket cinches the *direction* but explicitly leaves the *design leanings* open — there are six on record (from the pilot memo), each tagged "confirm or overturn at mount," plus two genuinely-open questions (the allocator, and the anchor naming/prefix under full mint discipline). That's a dense, multi-part deliverable.

**My recommendation:** trot it — walk the leanings and open questions one at a time, you pacing the advance, rather than my dropping all six plus the open questions in one wall. We'd land each as confirmed/overturned, then settle allocator and naming last since they depend on the earlier shape.

Want me to start the trot (first chunk: the category taxonomy and what "a third census mechanism" actually means structurally), or would you rather I lay out the full list of decision points first so you can see the whole board before we walk it?

content: We're designing the cited-constraint anchor — a spec-defined, code-cited mechanism for invariants and tripwires — in pace ₢BbAAO of heat ₣Bb. I've oriented and proposed trotting the design. Next: you pick whether to start the trot or see the full decision board first. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: trot please

content.text:
Trotting it. Six chunks, dependency-ordered so each builds on the last:

1. **The structural frame** — what "a third census mechanism" actually is, and the load-bearing decision: new MCM category vs. quoin variant
2. **The category taxonomy** — the kinds of cited constraint (invariant / foreign-signature / deliberate-deviation / membrane)
3. **The ID design** — opaque vs. readable, and why the security boundary drives it
4. **The allocator** — how IDs get minted collision-free (open question)
5. **The naming & prefix** — the anchor prefix and category words under mint discipline (open question)
6. **The validator rules** — what this mechanism feeds, and per-category obligations

You pace it — "next" advances, and a stray thought mid-walk gets its own discussion before we resume. Here's chunk 1.

---

## Chunk 1 of 6 — the structural frame

**Grounding from scratch** (this leans on two MCM mechanisms you authored but may not have line-read recently):

- A **quoin** is MCM's catalogued identifier for a *concept*: it has three parts — an attribute reference registered in a document's mapping section, an `[[anchor]]` at the one place the concept is defined, and the definition text. Because every reference resolves to that one anchor, a single grep finds the definition and every place the concept is invoked. It makes *concepts* countable.

- A **sprue** is the minted namespace for the *keys of one RB-authored JSON wire format* (e.g. `rblv_` for a lode-vouch envelope). One wire format, one sprue; foreign schemas keep their foreign keys. Because every producer and consumer of that wire format spells the keys with the same minted prefix, one grep finds every site that writes or reads them. It makes *wire keys* countable.

The thread I read across both — and the sense in which "census" is being used — is this: **each is a minting discipline that makes a whole class of cross-corpus thing enumerable by a single grep, because every instance shares a minted, greppable token.** Quoin censuses concepts; sprue censuses wire keys.

(I'm reconstructing "census" as that common property — I don't believe it has a formal MCM quoin home yet. If we keep the word load-bearing in the spec, minting it is itself a small decision that surfaces in chunk 5. Flagging now, not deciding now.)

**The cited constraint would be the third:** it makes *normative propositions* countable. A spec states a proposition once under a minted ID — "enclave-internal sources must not reach the entry port via DNAT" — and then the defending code site cites that ID, and the attacking test cites it too. One grep on the ID returns the definition, every line of code that defends it, and every test that attacks it. No registry, no list to drift — the grep *is* the census.

**The one decision for this chunk** — and the docket cinches the answer's *direction* but asks me to confirm the mechanics: this is a **new MCM category, not a variant of the quoin.**

The reason it can't be a quoin variant: a quoin's identity *is* its meaning — `rbw_depot` names the depot concept, and the readable name is the point. A cited constraint needs the opposite property in its identity (chunk 3 is where that bites: the spec is closed-source, the code is released, so a *readable* anchor would leak security reasoning into open identifiers). A thing whose identity must be semantics-free cannot be a sub-species of a thing whose identity must be semantic. They diverge at the root, so they're siblings in the census family, not parent-child.

That's the structural claim: **a new top-level census category in MCM, peer to quoin and sprue, defined at the MCM/AXLA home.** Everything downstream (taxonomy, ID shape, validator) hangs off accepting that framing.

Does the third-census-mechanism framing land, and do you confirm "new category, not quoin variant"? Say **next** when you're ready for the taxonomy.

lastPrompt: trot please

message.content: Yes, lets work on a new category, with a clever name like quoin/sprue (and I think there is another already, see if you spot it in MCM for context).  Next?

content.text: Good — let me hunt for the one you're pointing at before I take the taxonomy. You said there's already a third member of this family in MCM.

content.text:
Found it — and it's a better catch than the docket lets on.

## The third member: the **inlay** (`mcm_inlay`)

The docket says "a third census mechanism beside quoin and sprue" — but MCM actually already carries **three**, graded by *how much cataloguing apparatus the identifier carries*:

- **Quoin** — full apparatus: attribute reference in the mapping section, anchor at the definition site, definition text. Addressable from anywhere, tracked across the constellation.
- **Inlay** — prefix-disciplined but **uncatalogued**: no attribute reference, no anchor, no mapping-section entry. "Set permanently into a surface — recognized by its prefix pattern" — scoped to one subdocument, for hierarchy markers that need a formal identity without being elevated to quoin status.
- **Sprue** — minimal: a literal wire-level token in backticks, not even prefix-disciplined necessarily, recognized by its delimiters; may originate from foreign schemas.

So the real family axis isn't "census" loosely — it's a **gradient of cataloguing**: full (quoin) → prefix-only (inlay) → literal token (sprue).

**This sharpens where the cited constraint sits.** It's not "a third beside two" — it's a **fourth**, and it doesn't slot onto the cataloguing gradient at all. It's distinguished on a *different* axis: all three existing members identify a *name* (a concept, a marker, a wire key), whereas the cited constraint identifies a **proposition** — a statement that can be true or false, defended or attacked. And its references reach *out of the document constellation into released code and tests*, under an *opaque* ID. That's what makes it genuinely new rather than a quoin variant — confirmed, and now confirmed against the full family, not a partial view of it.

I'm banking "inlay" for chunk 5: the naming register is now quoin / inlay / sprue, and I'll want the fourth word to sit in that same set-into-a-surface / casting-and-construction register.

---

## Chunk 2 of 6 — the category taxonomy

**Context reminder:** a cited constraint names a *proposition*, but not all propositions are the same kind of thing. The category says *what kind of normative claim this is* — and the payoff is that the validator (chunk 6) can hang a **per-category obligation** on each kind. So the test of a good taxonomy isn't "does it describe the specimens" — it's "does each category imply a distinct validation duty." Categories that don't differ in obligation should collapse.

The pilot memo sketches four. Grounding each from scratch, with the sentry specimen it came from:

- **invariant** — a property that must always hold. *"Enclave-internal sources must not reach the entry port via DNAT."* Obligation: names a defender (the code that enforces it) and ideally an attacker (the test that tries to violate it).
- **deliberate-deviation** — a choice that *looks* wrong or simplifiable but is load-bearing. *"Loose rp_filter is deliberate; hardening it to strict silently kills entry-port traffic before the firewall sees it."* Obligation: names the **seductive wrong fix** — what an editor would "improve" that breaks it.
- **foreign-signature** — a characterized behavior of an ungovernable neighbor at the Palisade. *"Docker assigns the default route to the alphabetically-first network's gateway; compose `priority` is not honored."* Obligation: states the exact surveyed signature, never "it's flaky."
- **membrane** — a Palisade *workaround* that bends our rules to absorb a foreign behavior, carrying a demolition date. Obligation: names its **retirement condition** (the neighbor-healed grievance that retires it).

**The organizing insight I'd add** (and it's load-bearing, not decoration): these four split cleanly along the ROE's own recognition test — *can I edit the source of the failure?*

- **Interior / sovereign** (yes, it's ours): **invariant** + **deliberate-deviation** — properties we assert and defend.
- **Palisade / edge** (no, it's a neighbor's): **foreign-signature** + **membrane** — the characterization, and the containment built against it.

That split isn't just tidy — it's the reason the taxonomy is principled rather than a grab-bag, and it gives a place to put a *fifth* category later without re-deriving (it'll land on one side of that line).

**Two honest flags before you rule:**

1. **`membrane` is speculative** — the sentry pair had zero specimens; it's a predicted category. Minting a category ahead of seeing one carries a small risk of designing for an imagined shape. I lean toward keeping it (it falls straight out of ROE rule 5, so it's not invented from nothing), but it's the one I'd be readiest to defer-until-first-specimen if you want the taxonomy to stay strictly evidence-backed.
2. **`foreign-signature` and `deliberate-deviation` co-occur** — one sentry comment (F1) was both: Docker's behavior (signature) *and* our refusal to trust it (deviation). That's fine — it means one code site can cite two anchors — but it confirms they're genuinely distinct obligations rather than one fuzzy category.

The decision for this chunk: **confirm the four** (with the interior/Palisade organizing split), or collapse/defer `membrane`, or add a kind I'm missing. Say **next** when you've ruled.

content.text:
You're right — I made each chunk its own wall. Stripping it down.

A **cited constraint** is just a rule the spec writes down once, that code and tests point back to.

Rules come in a few kinds. From the pilot, I found four:

1. **Must-be-true** — "X always holds."
2. **Looks-wrong, don't touch** — "this odd-looking code is deliberate."
3. **How the outside behaves** — "Docker does this weird thing we can't change."
4. **Temporary workaround** — "remove this when the upstream bug is fixed."

Do these four feel like the right kinds? I have reasoning behind each, but I'll keep it in my pocket unless you ask.

message.content: Good heavens they look brilliant!  Proceed!

content.text:
## Chunk 3 of 6 — what the ID looks like

**The question:** when code points back to a rule, what does the pointer look like — a readable name like `sentry_dnat_rule`, or an opaque tag like `Xk7q`?

**The catch that decides it:** the spec is private, but the code ships to the public. A readable name in public code leaks the security reasoning — an attacker reading our released code would learn exactly what we're defending and why. An opaque tag tells an outsider nothing, while our own grep still finds every site.

So: **the ID is an opaque tag, not a readable word.** The plain-English rule still lives where our people read it (the spec, plus a short note in the code). Only the *pointer* is opaque.

One opaque tag, meaningless to a stranger, greppable to us. Does that sit right? Say **next** for chunk 4 (how those tags get handed out without collisions).

content: We're designing the "cited constraint" — a spec rule that code and tests point back to by an opaque tag. We've settled the new category, the four rule-kinds, and opaque IDs. Next: how tags get assigned without collisions. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: opaque... Didn't we agree on a 'safe' mixed case prefix to make the grep unique?

content.text:
Yes — exactly that, and I conflated two things by calling it "opaque." Let me separate them:

- The **prefix** is a deliberate **mixed-case** tag (something like `RBx…`) whose whole job is a clean grep — lowercase `rbs*` names already exist in the repo, so a mixed-case prefix *can't* collide with them. One grep, zero false hits.
- The **tail** is the opaque part — meaningless to a stranger.

So a full ID reads like `RBx7q4`: mixed-case prefix for unique grep, opaque tail so it leaks nothing. (The mixed case is a deliberate break from the usual snake_case anchor convention — worth recording as intentional when we write it up.)

The exact prefix letters I'll settle in chunk 5 under full mint discipline. Does separating it that way square with what you remembered? Then **next** takes us to chunk 4 — handing out those tails without two people minting the same one.

message.content: I still want an '_' or _maybe_ a '-' to make these sprue/quoin rhymed...

content.text:
Good call — the separator is what makes it read as *family* rather than a random token.

So: `RBx_7q4`, not `RBx7q4` — prefix, separator, opaque tail. Same shape as `mcm_quoin` and `rbw_depot`, just an opaque body where they have a readable word. That's the rhyme.

On which separator — I'd take `_` over `-`:

- `_` is the identifier separator *everywhere* in the repo: quoin prefixes, anchors, variable names all use it. An underscore makes the new ID sit visually inside the same family.
- `-` already carries other jobs: kebab-case silks, tabtarget delimiters. Reusing it here would quietly clash.

So `_` rhymes; `-` would fight existing meaning. Lock the underscore? Then **next** takes us to chunk 4 — handing out the opaque tails without collisions.

message.content: Good, '_' then.  Yes, next...  I honestly am good with randomized or time encoded but verified; know in the future we will have linting tools to enforce this, but until then, an ACG written, LLM interpreted allocate/verify-unique cycle sits with me well.

lastPrompt: Good, '_' then.  Yes, next...  I honestly am good with randomized or time encoded but verified; know in the future we will have linting tools to enforce this, but until then, an ACG written, LLM inter…

content.text:
Locked. So the allocator, until tooling exists, is a **procedure written into ACG** — not a program:

1. Generate a tail — random or time-based, your choice; both work.
2. Grep the repo for the full ID.
3. Zero hits → it's yours. Any hit → regenerate.

What I like about your instinct here: it keeps the "no registry to drift" property intact. There's no central list of allocated IDs — **the grep is both the uniqueness check and the census**, the same grep doing both jobs. When real linting arrives later, it enforces exactly this invariant; the ACG procedure is just the human/LLM bridge until then. (Random vs. time-encoded makes no difference to correctness since both get grep-verified — I'd lean random as the slightly more inert of the two, but it's a coin-flip.)

**next** takes us to chunk 5 — the actual name: the family word (the clever quoin/sprue/inlay-register word for this mechanism) and the mixed-case prefix letters.

content: We're designing the cited-constraint anchor: a spec-stated rule that code and tests grep back to. Settled so far: new MCM category, opaque tail behind a mixed-case prefix with an underscore, grep-verified allocation. Next: pick the family name and prefix letters (chunk 5 of 6).

Grep gate: **all clean, zero hits** — every candidate is mintable. So this is purely about which word does the best semantic work.

My lead: **rivet**. A rivet is permanent and load-bearing — *"don't pull a rivet"* is exactly the don't-touch heart of these constraints — and it pairs with sprue's metalworking register. Code and tests "rivet to" the rule.

message.content: How did you choose the phrasing 'locked'?  I recall context document edits where we started using the word 'cinch' instead of 'lock' in order to disambiguate against 'software locks'.  Do we have some trailing references?

content.text:
Good catch — and an honest answer: I reached for "locked" reflexively. It's a *trodden word* (ambient software prose), and "cinch" is precisely the membrane this collaboration built against that reflex — decisions are **cinched**, and "lock" is reserved for the concurrency-sense git-ref commit lock. I slipped into the very usage the convention exists to override. Should have said "cinched" every time above.

Let me check for trailing references — stale "lock"-as-decided uses that should be "cinch."

content.text:
Triaged — and the guides/specs are clean of the decided-sense. Every hit is a *different, legitimate* meaning of "lock":

- **Concurrency / commit lock** (the word it keeps): VOSRL-lock, VOSRC-commit, VOS0 `:vosr_lock:`, RBSTR "no external lock," RBS0 "git's internal locking," AXLA "seed incremented under lock."
- **BCG readonly lock-step** (a named mechanism): BCG's kindle→enforce→**lock**, RBSRR/RBSRA "at lock time," "locks the regime against further modification."
- **Security / OS lockdown**: the garrison/caparison `command=` "locked-down," "lock out auth," RBSIP "subsystem being locked down," RBS0 "egress-locked build."
- **External pinning/state**: RBSHR "retention-locked GCS," RBSLI "locked version," RBSPV "locked to a Podman version," RBSCB skopeo issue "(locked)."
- **Trodden-word list**: MCM:1172 names "lock" itself as disqualified.

**Zero `## Locked` headings, zero "lock the approach / locked decision" strays.** The cinch convention holds everywhere it could have leaked.

Two soft flags, both defensible — your call whether they're worth a touch someday, not now:

1. **MCM:1275** — the quoin definition says the cornerstone "*locks* the structure." Pure physical metaphor, but a small irony given MCM itself bans "lock" as a minted term two pages earlier.
2. **RBSCIP:190** — "values *locked in* `rbgc_constants.sh`" — means "frozen in constants," not a re-litigable decision, so it's not really cinch-territory — but it's the closest phrasing to the decided-sense in the whole corpus.

I did **not** sweep the JJK specs (JJS0/JJSC*) or the lenses — JJS0 is the commit-lock's home, so it's dense with legitimate references. Want me to run the same pass there to fully close it, or are those two soft flags enough and we resume the trot at chunk 6?

content.text:
JJK specs: **every hit is the commit lock** — `refs/vvg/locks/vvx` acquire/release, `lock → load → transform → save → unlock`, "commit lock serializes," "seed incremented under commit lock," "read-only, no lock." Pure concurrency sense, the word it keeps. **Zero decided-sense strays.**

So the full sweep is done — context docs, guides, RB/CMK/BUK/VOK/APCK specs, and JJK specs all clean. The cinch convention held everywhere; the only stray "locked" in this whole exercise was mine, live. Resuming the trot.

---

## Chunk 6 of 6 — where the kind lives, and what the validator checks

**Context reminder:** back in chunk 2 we settled four *kinds* of rivet (must-be-true / looks-wrong-don't-touch / how-the-outside-behaves / temporary-workaround). Two questions remain: does the kind show up *in the ID*, and what would a checker enforce?

**The kind is recorded at the definition site — not in the ID.** A rivet ID is just `RBr_7q4`; it carries no kind-letter. The kind is a field on the rivet's *definition* in the spec. Why: if you later decide a rivet is really a "don't-touch" rather than a "must-be-true," you just change the field — the ID never re-mints, so no code or test citation goes stale. Same principle as a firemark: identity carries no meaning, so meaning can change without breaking the identity.

**What the future checker enforces** (the ACG grep-procedure is the human stand-in until this tool exists):

- **The core check:** every `RBr_…` cited in code or tests resolves to exactly one spec definition. No dangling pointers, no double-defines.
- **One duty per kind:**
  - *must-be-true* → must point at the code that enforces it (ideally a test that attacks it too)
  - *looks-wrong, don't touch* → must state the tempting wrong "fix"
  - *how the outside behaves* → must state the exact behavior, precisely
  - *temporary workaround* → must name when it gets removed

That's the whole mechanism. The trot's done — all six chunks settled.

---

Where I'd go next is **writing it up**, which is what this pace's "Done when" actually asks for: define `mcm_rivet` at the MCM home, repoint ACG's deferred section to live, and record the decisions. Two ways to take it:

- **Compile first** — I lay out all six decisions as a tight summary for you to eyeball before I touch any file, *or*
- **Draft first** — I go straight to the MCM definition + ACG repoint and you review the diffs.

Which? (I won't wrap the pace until you say so.)

message.content: I am vaguely recalling a chat where we were leaning that rivet signatures would hide their type but that we would have AXLA voicings for them clearly stating their types.  Any concerns with this approach?

content.text:
No real concern — and it's actually *better* than the bespoke "field" I described in chunk 6.

One-line grounding: an **AXLA voicing** is a small annotation at a definition site saying "this plays role X" (like `//axk_premise` marks a definition as a design-constraint premise); the roles themselves — **motifs** — live in AXLA, the shared cross-model vocabulary.

So your recalled approach is: the rivet ID in code stays opaque (`RBr_7q4`, leaks nothing), but the rivet's **definition in the spec** carries a voicing naming its kind.

**Why that beats a plain field:** the voicing lives in the *spec*, which is private. So the kind is stated *clearly* — but only on the closed side of the boundary. The public code still sees only the opaque ID. A code-side field couldn't pull that off; the voicing is the cleaner fit for the exact security reason we chose opacity in the first place. So this *refines chunk 6*: the "kind field" becomes an AXLA voicing — same definition-site home, existing machinery, better placement.

Three small things to settle at write-up (none blockers):

1. **Voicings currently attach to quoins** — a rivet is a new category, not a quoin. We generalize "voicing applies to a *definition site*" (rivets and quoins both have one). Tiny.
2. **The four kinds become AXLA motifs** — a real mint in the cross-model layer. "Must-be-true" is plainly universal; "how-the-outside-behaves" and "temporary-workaround" ride the Palisade/ROE, which is universal to how we work. Fits.
3. **"Temporary-workaround" is still the speculative one** — minting an AXLA motif for a kind we've never actually seen is a bigger commitment than a local field would've been. Cleanest: voice it *lazily* — mint that motif when the first one actually appears.

Want me to fold the voicing approach into the write-up plan? It mostly just edits chunk 6's "field" into "voicing."

content.text:
Good poke — I was wrong, and the correction reshapes the design slightly.

**Corrected:** voicings are *not* quoin-specific. They're **definition-site annotations**, and AXLA already runs several *families* of them — regime (`axvr_`), operation (`axvo_`), entity (`axve_`), operation-output (`axpo_`). So adding a family for rivets isn't new machinery; it's slotting into an established pattern — which actually *lowers* the cost of the whole mechanism.

**Do we have sprue voicings? No — and the reason is structural.** A sprue isn't a *voiced subject*; it's a **lookahead argument**. When a term gets voiced (say a wire member: `//axl_voices axr_member axd_required axt_string`), the voicing carries a *sprue lookahead* that binds the literal backtick wire-key sitting next to it. The sprue is the thing a voicing *reads*, not a thing that *gets* voiced. Inlays are the same — lookahead targets, not voiced subjects.

That hands us a cleaner family cut than the "cataloguing gradient" I drew in chunk 1:

- **Voiced subjects** — have a definition site that gets annotated: **quoin**, and now **rivet**.
- **Lookahead arguments** — literal tokens a voicing reads: **inlay**, **sprue**.

A rivet sits on the quoin side — it has a real definition with definition text, so it voices like a quoin.

**So, your two:**

- **Voicing types on rivets — yes.** Mint a new `axv…_` family; the four kinds are its terms. Fits the pattern, and it lands the kind on the *private spec side* — the security win we were after. One discipline note: check whether an existing motif already covers a kind before minting four fresh ones.

- **Voicing types on sprues — I'd resist.** A sprue's type/required/arity facts already live on the voiced term it hangs off. A standalone "sprue voicing" would be a *second* home for a fact that already has one — the exact dedup violation this heat exists to kill. The asymmetry (rivet voiced like a quoin; sprue stays a lookahead target) is **load-bearing**, not an inconsistency to repair.

The one thing that would flip me on sprues: do you have a *homeless* sprue fact in mind — something true about a sprue with nowhere to live today? If so, say what it is and I'll reconsider.

lastPrompt: Fair question... Are you sure we don't have sprue voicings?  Thoughts on adding voicing types to rivets and sprues?

message.content: A really useful sprue concept are json strings.  When I control them (inside the palisade?) they indeed often are 'homeless' unless we get an asciidoc subdocument to own them.  They are at least locatable until we need to have them have a home.  I think we also en-sprued the rbi_ ideas to clearly label all GAR resident types, though I can't remember if we did AXLA tie them.  THoughts?

content.text: This is the homeless-fact case I asked for — and it lands. Let me check the `rbi_` AXLA-tie status before I answer from memory, since that's the crux.
